    High-Redshift Galaxies

    High-Redshift Galaxies

    Type:Galaxy population
    Level:Intermediate
    Redshift range:z > 6 (cosmic ages < 1 Gyr)
    Key facility:JWST (NIRSpec, NIRCam, MIRI)
    Related: Cosmic dawn
    Red monsters
    Blue monsters
    UV slope
    Star formation

    High-redshift galaxies are systems observed at redshifts z > 6, corresponding to cosmic ages less than ~1 billion years after the Big Bang. Their study probes the earliest phases of galaxy formation, the buildup of stellar mass, the production of heavy elements and dust, and the process of cosmic reionization. The launch of JWST in 2022 revolutionized this field, revealing an unexpectedly abundant and diverse population of galaxies at z > 10[1][2][3].

    The JWST Revolution at z > 10

    Prior to JWST, galaxies beyond z ~ 10 were largely inaccessible. JWST’s infrared sensitivity has uncovered a surprisingly luminous population at z = 10–16, with UV luminosity functions exceeding pre-launch model predictions[1][2]. Most of these galaxies exhibit extremely blue UV slopes (βUV < −2.5), consistent with young, metal-poor stellar populations with minimal dust[4][5]. Their high specific star formation rates suggest rapid early assembly[6].

    Population Diversity: Red and Blue

    While the majority of z > 10 galaxies are blue and dust-poor, JWST has revealed a minority population of red UV continuum sources that deviate significantly from the canonical MUV–βUV relation[7][8]. These objects — termed “red monsters” in the Ferrara+2024 framework[9] — show red UV slopes (βUV > −1.5) indicative of significant dust attenuation. The spectroscopic confirmation of EGS-z11-R0 at z = 11.45 with AV ~ 1 mag established that chemically evolved, dust-enriched galaxies were already in place at cosmic dawn[10].

    The coexistence of blue and red populations supports an evolutionary picture in which galaxies alternate between dust-obscured and dust-free phases, regulated by radiative feedback[9]. This diversity has direct implications for estimating the cosmic star formation rate density at z > 10, as UV-selected samples may miss heavily obscured systems[10].

    Physical Properties

    SED modeling of z > 10 galaxies reveals stellar masses ranging from log(M*/M⊙) ~ 7 to 10, with some systems achieving surprisingly high masses (> 109 M⊙) within only ~400 Myr[6][10]. Star formation rates span ~1–40 M⊙ yr−1, with rising or constant star formation histories preferred over instantaneous bursts in many cases[10]. Emission-line diagnostics reveal hard ionizing radiation fields, with UV line ratios (C IV/C III], C III]/He II) often falling in composite AGN/SF regions — reflecting a fundamental diagnostic degeneracy at these redshifts[10][11][12].

    Early Chemical Enrichment

    High-redshift galaxies show surprisingly rapid chemical enrichment. JWST/NIRSpec detections of iron emission lines at z > 10 demonstrate that iron-peak elements — key constituents of dust grains — were already present in the ISM within ~400 Myr[10][13]. Stellar iron abundances at z = 9–12 range from [Fe/H] ~ −1 to solar, indicating that multiple generations of massive stars and supernovae had already enriched the gas[13]. This rapid enrichment supports models of prompt dust production and grain growth.

    See Also

    • Cosmic dawn — the earliest observable epoch
    • Red monsters — dust-obscured early galaxies
    • Blue monsters — UV-bright, dust-free early galaxies
    • UV slope — key observable for characterizing high-z stellar populations
    • MUV–βUV relation — canonical UV colour-magnitude relation
    • Rodighiero et al. (2026) — spectroscopic confirmation of a red monster at z = 11.45

    References

    1. Naidu, R. P. et al. (2022). "Two remarkably luminous galaxy candidates at z ≈ 10–12." ApJ, 940, L14.
    2. Harikane, Y. et al. (2022). "A search for H-dropout Lyman Break Galaxies at z ~ 12–16." ApJ, 929, 1.
    3. Castellano, M. et al. (2023). "Early JWST galaxy discoveries."
    4. Morales, A. M. et al. (2024). "UV slope measurements at z > 10."
    5. Cullen, F. et al. (2024). "UV slope-magnitude relation at z ~ 10–12."
    6. Santini, P. et al. (2025). "High stellar masses and sSFR at z > 10 indicating rapid assembly."
    7. Rodighiero, G. et al. (2023). "Discovery of red UV continuum candidates at high redshift."
    8. Mitsuhashi, I. et al. (2025). "Red candidates at z ~ 12 deviating from the MUV–βUV relation."
    9. Ferrara, A. (2024). "Red Monsters and Blue Monsters: radiative feedback and the early growth of galaxies."
    10. Rodighiero, G. et al. (2026). "EGS-z11-R0: a red, dust-rich galaxy at Cosmic Dawn." arXiv:2603.15841
    11. Bunker, A. J. et al. (2023). "JADES NIRSpec spectroscopy of GN-z11." A&A, 677, A88.
    12. Napolitano, L. et al. (2025). "GHZ9: composite AGN+stellar analysis at z = 10.145."
    13. Nakane, M. et al. (2025). "Stellar iron abundances at z = 9–12 from NIRSpec spectra."
